# Vertically Integrated Healthcare Price Transparency

> Most companies stop at one layer. We built the entire infrastructure — from large-scale healthcare price transparency datasets, pricing transparency data APIs to expert-led payer contract negotiations.

## Benchmarks

- [BenchMarks | The complete healthcare pricing insights platform. | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/benchmarks): BenchMarks is an easy-to-use platform that empowers revenue cycle teams with comprehensive CPT code rate comparisons across providers and hospitals. Make data-driven decisions, optimize negotiations, and ensure competitive pricing based on actual negotiated rates.

## Compliance-consulting-navigate-cms-pricing-transparency-mandates-with-confidence-sumhealth

- [Compliance Consulting | Navigate CMS Pricing Transparency Mandates with Confidence | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/compliance-consulting-navigate-cms-pricing-transparency-mandates-with-confidence-sumhealth): SumHealth helps hospitals achieve full CMS pricing transparency compliance. Avoid penalties, streamline data, and build patient trust with our expert solutions. Get a free compliance assessment!

## Contact

- [Let's Talk | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/contact): Connect with SumHealth for personalized assistance and support. Our contact page provides a direct line to our team, ensuring a smooth experience for patients, providers, and healthcare systems.

## Contract-strategy-reimbursement-optimization

- [Contract Strategy & Reimbursement Optimization | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/contract-strategy-reimbursement-optimization): In today's concentrated healthcare market, small variations in contracted rates translate to millions in lost revenue. Our Market Assessment and Payer Contract Strategy service provides the data, leverage, and roadmap you need to secure top-tier reimbursement and financial stability

## Feedback

- [Feedback | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/feedback): Leave your feedback on our platform and contribute to the future of healthcare. Your reviews matter as we strive to simplify the healthcare experience for all—patients, providers, and healthcare systems.

## Hospital-bill-negotiation

- [Hospital Bill Negotiation | Save Up to 100% | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/hospital-bill-negotiation): Expert hospital bill negotiation that saves you money. We use real hospital pricing data to reduce bills by up to 100%. No upfront costs - you only pay if we save you money.

## Hospital-pricing-api

- [Hospital Pricing Data API | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/hospital-pricing-api): Harness the power of payer and hospital price transparency data. Get the latest negotiated rates for any healthcare provider nationwide. Customize payers and CPT codes to fit your specific needs. Ideal for hospitals, startups, and healthcare enterprises.

## Hospital-pricing-tool

- [Search Prices for Hospital Procedures | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/hospital-pricing-tool): Explore pricing data from 1,500+ hospitals nationwide - free access. Compare costs and make informed decisions today.

## Main

- [Vertically Integrated Healthcare Price Transparency | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org): Most companies stop at one layer. We built the entire infrastructure — from large-scale healthcare price transparency datasets, pricing transparency data APIs to expert-led payer contract negotiations.
- [Vertically Integrated Healthcare Price Transparency | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/#): Most companies stop at one layer. We built the entire infrastructure — from large-scale healthcare price transparency datasets, pricing transparency data APIs to expert-led payer contract negotiations.

## Payer-data

- [Payer Price Transparency Data | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/payer-data): Harness the power of payer price transparency data. Get the latest negotiated rates for any healthcare provider nationwide. Customize payers and CPT codes to fit your specific needs. Ideal for hospitals, startups, and healthcare enterprises.

## Post

- [A Quick Glimpse into Healthcare Price Transparency Efforts](https://sumhealth.org/post/a-quick-glimpse-into-healthcare-price-transparency-efforts): Discover the latest efforts pushing for greater healthcare price transparency. This post explores recent legislation, initiatives, and innovative tech solutions like SumHealth's platform that are empowering patients with clear pricing data.
- [An Analysis of Payer Machine-Readable Files (MRFs) and the Challenges of Healthcare Price Transparency](https://sumhealth.org/post/an-analysis-of-payer-machine-readable-files-mrfs-and-the-challenges-of-healthcare-price-transparency): Explore the complexities of payer MRFs: size, compliance, and data challenges. Learn how to navigate these files for healthcare price transparency and discover solutions.
- [Hospital Price Transparency 2025: Your Complete Compliance Guide to Avoid Millions in Penalties](https://sumhealth.org/post/hospital-price-transparency-2025-your-complete-compliance-guide-to-avoid-millions-in-penalties): With only 21% of hospitals compliant with 2025 price transparency rules, non-compliant facilities face $5.5M penalties. This guide provides urgent 90-day roadmap to avoid fines and public exposure.
- [February 2024 Hospital Price Transparency Report Reveals Widespread Noncompliance](https://sumhealth.org/post/hospital-price-transparency-in-2024-key-insights-from-the-latest-compliance-report): Discover the latest findings on hospital price transparency compliance rates as of February 2024. Explore why only 34.5% of reviewed hospitals are fully compliant, while the majority face challenges with missing or incomplete pricing data.
- [How AI Is Transforming Healthcare](https://sumhealth.org/post/how-ai-is-transforming-healthcare): Artificial intelligence (AI) and Generative AI are on track to adding trillions of dollars to the global economy through an increase in worker productivity by 0.1% to 0.6% into 2040 according to statistics by McKinsey. In particular, we see the impact of AI on global healthcare, offering solutions from advanced diagnostics to the management of chronic conditions.
- [Price Transparency: Bringing Clarity to the Cost of Health](https://sumhealth.org/post/price-transparency-bringing-clarity-to-the-cost-of-health): Beyond empowering patients to make informed choices, our price transparency platform assists healthcare providers in navigating compliance with regulatory standards, avoiding substantial fines associated with non-compliance.
- [The Current State of Hospital Price Transparency in February 2025](https://sumhealth.org/post/the-current-state-of-hospital-price-transparency-in-february-2025): Hospital price transparency has become a cornerstone of healthcare reform in the United States, with new regulations taking effect on January 1, 2025. These rules aim to enhance consumer access to pricing information, promote competition, and reduce healthcare costs. However, challenges in compliance and usability persist. This post is an overview of the current state of hospital price transparency.
- [Why Price Transparency Data is Non-Negotiable for Your Self-Insured Plan](https://sumhealth.org/post/why-price-transparency-is-non-negotiable-for-your-self-insured-plan): Is your self-insured plan truly optimized? Discover how leveraging healthcare price transparency data can unlock massive savings and give you a competitive edge.

## Privacy-policy

- [Privacy Policy | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/privacy-policy): Trust is paramount in healthcare. Dive into SumHealth's comprehensive privacy policy, ensuring the confidentiality and security of your data. We prioritize your privacy as we streamline healthcare for patients, providers, and healthcare systems.

## Redefining-proactive-health-sumhealth-nucleus-genomics

- [Redefining Proactive Health | Sumhealth + Nucleus Genomics](https://sumhealth.org/redefining-proactive-health-sumhealth-nucleus-genomics): SumHealth and Nucleus Genomics are combining our expertise to transform how people and providers use genetic information. We believe that understanding your DNA is a powerful tool for informed health decisions.

## Resources

- [Resources | Healthcare Insights, Briefs and Report](https://sumhealth.org/resources): Stay informed with the latest insights, in-depth briefs and comprehensive reports on the dynamic healthcare industry from SumHealth's expert analysts. Explore emerging trends, legislative updates, market analysis and more.

## Trademark

- [Trademark](https://sumhealth.org/trademark): SumHealth™ is our registered trademark. Learn about our brand's protection and proper usage guidelines for the SumHealth™ name and logo.

## Truecharge-ai-bill-repricer

- [TrueCharge AI | Drag & Drop Hospital Bill Repricer | SumHealth](https://sumhealth.org/truecharge-ai-bill-repricer): TrueCharge AI instantly analyzes hospital bills against our comprehensive database of median pricing, giving your team the leverage they need to secure better outcomes for your clients.

# A Quick Glimpse into Healthcare Price Transparency Efforts
URL: https://sumhealth.org/post/a-quick-glimpse-into-healthcare-price-transparency-efforts

A Quick Glimpse into Healthcare Price Transparency EffortsMarch 18, 2024Share to your network For too long, patients in the United States have been left in the dark when it comes to understanding the true costs of healthcare services. The lack of price transparency has been a major contributor to rising healthcare costs and patient frustration. However, recent efforts and legislation aim to lift the veil on medical billing practices, empowering consumers with crucial pricing information.‍GroundworkThe push for healthcare price transparency gained momentum in the late 2010s. The Hospital Price Transparency rule, which took effect in January 2021, required hospitals to disclose their negotiated rates with insurers and the cash prices for various services. While this was a step in the right direction, the rule faced challenges in implementation and enforcement, leaving many patients still perplexed by the complex maze of healthcare pricing.‍Existing Legislation and InitiativesIn addition to the Hospital Price Transparency rule, several other legislative efforts and initiatives have aimed to improve price transparency in the healthcare sector:‍The No Surprises Act (2022):This legislation aimed to protect patients from unexpected medical bills from out-of-network providers, particularly in emergency situations.It also required healthcare providers to furnish patients with good-faith estimates of expected charges for scheduled services.‍State-Level Efforts:Several states, such as Colorado, Connecticut, and Nevada, have implemented their own price transparency laws, requiring healthcare providers to disclose pricing information to consumers.Insurer Transparency Initiatives:Some insurance companies have started to provide pricing tools and cost estimators to their members, allowing them to compare costs for various services across different providers.‍Potential Impact and ChallengesImproved healthcare price transparency has the potential to empower patients and drive down costs through increased competition and informed decision-making. When armed with pricing information, consumers can shop around for the most affordable options, putting pressure on healthcare providers to offer competitive rates.However, implementing effective transparency measures has proven challenging. Resistance from industry stakeholders, concerns about the accuracy and usability of pricing information, and the sheer complexity of healthcare billing practices have all presented obstacles.‍Moving ForwardAs the push for healthcare price transparency continues, policymakers, industry leaders, and consumer advocates must work together to address the remaining challenges. Efforts should focus on ensuring pricing information is not only disclosed but also presented in a clear, standardized, and easily understandable manner for patients.Ultimately, greater transparency in healthcare costs is crucial for building a more consumer-friendly and affordable healthcare system. By shining a light on often-hidden expenses, patients can make informed decisions about their care and take control of their healthcare expenses.‍‍Our Contribution to Healthcare Cost Transparency‍At SumHealth, we recognize the growing importance of healthcare price transparency and the need to empower patients with clear, accessible pricing information. By bringing pricing data into the open and making it easily searchable, we aim to bridge the information gap and contribute to greater cost clarity within the healthcare industry.Our platform represents a technological solution to a long-standing problem – the lack of transparency around healthcare costs. As the push for price transparency continues to gain momentum, SumHealth is proud to be at the forefront, leveraging technology to put powerful pricing information directly into the hands of patients and caregivers.We encourage you to stay tuned for updates on the consumer launch of our hospital price data platform. # An Analysis of Payer Machine-Readable Files (MRFs) and the Challenges of Healthcare Price Transparency
URL: https://sumhealth.org/post/an-analysis-of-payer-machine-readable-files-mrfs-and-the-challenges-of-healthcare-price-transparency

An Analysis of Payer Machine-Readable Files (MRFs) and the Challenges of Healthcare Price TransparencySumHealth TeamMarch 6, 2025Share to your network The implementation of the Transparency in Coverage (TiC) rule, mandated by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), has introduced a paradigm shift in healthcare price transparency. At the core of this initiative are Machine-Readable Files (MRFs), comprehensive datasets published by health insurers detailing negotiated rates for in-network services. However, the practical application of these files has revealed significant challenges related to data volume, structural complexity, and compliance variability.Conceptual Framework: The Purpose of MRFsMRFs are designed to fulfill the TiC rule's objective of empowering consumers with access to price information, thereby facilitating informed healthcare decision-making. These files encompass:In-network rate files: Articulating negotiated reimbursement rates between insurers and contracted providers.Allowed amount files: Presenting out-of-network allowed amounts, indicating the insurer's determination of reasonable charges.Prescription drug files: Disclosing negotiated rates and historical net prices for pharmaceutical products.These files are intended to serve as a foundational resource for price discovery and comparative analysis.Quantitative Analysis: The Scale of Data VolumeA primary impediment to MRF utilization is the sheer magnitude of the data. Observations include:Data Set Size: Files routinely exceed gigabytes and, in some instances, terabytes, requiring substantial computational resources for processing.Variability in Scale: Data volume fluctuates significantly based on insurer size, network breadth, and the extent of negotiated rate agreements.Temporal Data Accumulation: Monthly updates compound the existing data load, necessitating scalable data management solutions.This quantitative scale presents a significant challenge for researchers and developers seeking to derive meaningful insights.Qualitative Assessment: Structural Complexity and Data HeterogeneityBeyond volume, the intrinsic complexity of MRF data structures poses a substantial barrier to effective analysis. Key challenges include:Data Format and Parsing: Files are typically formatted in JSON or other machine-readable formats, demanding specialized parsing capabilities.Lack of Data Standardization: While CMS provides guidelines, variations in data organization and nomenclature across insurers impede cross-insurer comparisons.Medical Coding and Terminology: The use of complex medical coding systems and terminology necessitates domain expertise for accurate interpretation.Granularity and Data Abstraction: While detailed data is provided, the level of granularity can obfuscate the overall cost of specific services, requiring sophisticated data abstraction techniques.Compliance Evaluation: Adherence to Regulatory StandardsCompliance with the TiC rule has exhibited variability across insurers. Observations include:Accessibility and Discoverability: Some files are not readily accessible or discoverable, hindering public access.Data Completeness and Accuracy: Instances of incomplete or inaccurate data have been reported, raising concerns about data integrity.Timeliness of Publication: Adherence to the mandated monthly publication schedule has proven challenging for some insurers.Regulatory Enforcement: The efficacy of regulatory enforcement mechanisms remains a subject of ongoing discussion.Implications and Future DirectionsThe TiC rule and MRFs hold the potential to transform healthcare price transparency. However, the current challenges necessitate concerted efforts to:Establish standardized data formats and nomenclature.Develop robust data analysis tools and platforms.Enhance regulatory oversight and enforcement.Promote interdisciplinary research to analyze the data and create useful tools.By addressing these challenges, we can realize the full potential of MRFs in fostering a more transparent and efficient healthcare market. Organizations seeking to navigate the complexities of payer data can leverage specialized services to streamline their analysis and decision-making processes. For instance, SumHealth provides comprehensive payer data services, offering expertise in accessing, processing, and interpreting MRF data. These services empower organizations to gain valuable insights into market trends, pricing dynamics, and competitive landscapes, ultimately facilitating informed strategic planning and operational efficiency. # February 2024 Hospital Price Transparency Report Reveals Widespread Noncompliance
URL: https://sumhealth.org/post/hospital-price-transparency-in-2024-key-insights-from-the-latest-compliance-report

February 2024 Hospital Price Transparency Report Reveals Widespread NoncomplianceApril 9, 2024Share to your network The latest Hospital Price Transparency Compliance Report, released in February 2024, sheds light on the progress and challenges in achieving meaningful price transparency in the healthcare industry. This report, compiled by PatientRightsAdvocate.org, analyzed the websites of 2,000 U.S. hospitals to assess their compliance with the federal Hospital Price Transparency Rule. Download the full report here.Key Highlights:1. Compliance Rates Remain Low: Only 34.5% of the hospitals reviewed were found to be fully compliant with all the requirements of the price transparency rule. The remaining 65.5% were deemed noncompliant due to missing or incomplete pricing data.2. Inconsistent Reporting: The report found significant variation in compliance among the nation's largest hospital systems. While some systems, like Community Health Systems and Christus Health, had high compliance rates, others, such as HCA Healthcare, Tenet Healthcare, and Kaiser Permanente, had no fully compliant hospitals.3. Barriers to Usability: Many hospitals posted pricing data in formats that were difficult for consumers and technology developers to navigate, such as unnecessarily large "tall" files and complex JSON-formatted files without user documentation.4. Lack of Enforcement: The report highlights the need for more thorough and timely enforcement of the price transparency rule by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS). To date, CMS has issued penalty notices to only 14 out of the approximately 1,800 hospitals it has identified as noncompliant.Insights and Recommendations:1. Strengthen Enforcement: The report calls for CMS to enforce the price transparency rule more quickly and thoroughly, publicly post the entire compliance process, and utilize payers' Transparency in Coverage data to verify the accuracy and completeness of hospitals' price files.2. Eliminate Price Estimates: The report suggests that CMS should require hospitals to provide actual, binding prices for each payer and plan, as well as discounted cash prices, rather than relying on non-binding price estimates.3. Standardize Pricing Data: The report recommends that CMS set additional requirements for the "tall" file format, including a maximum number of rows and a cap on file size, to improve the usability of the data.4. Require Executive Attestation: The report encourages CMS to require senior hospital officials, such as the Chief Executive Officer, to attest to the accuracy and completeness of the pricing data, potentially incorporating liability under the False Claims Act.5. Expand Transparency to All Providers: The report suggests that price transparency requirements should be expanded to all healthcare providers, including ambulatory surgical centers and accountable care organizations, to allow for more comprehensive price comparisons.SumHealth's Price Transparency Solution: Fast Track ComplianceSumHealth offers a dynamic price transparency solution, swiftly tailored to meet the unique needs of any hospital. # How AI Is Transforming Healthcare
URL: https://sumhealth.org/post/how-ai-is-transforming-healthcare

How AI Is Transforming HealthcareZara AhmedMay 8, 2024Share to your network Artificial intelligence (AI) and Generative AI are on track to adding trillions of dollars to the global economy through an increase in worker productivity by 0.1% to 0.6% into 2040 according to statistics by McKinsey. In particular, we see the impact of AI on global healthcare, offering solutions from advanced diagnostics to the management of chronic conditions. 38% of Americans think AI usage in healthcare would improve patient outcomes according to a Pew Research Center Survey. Here are six ways that AI is reshaping global healthcare systems, and reducing pressure on them due to supply chain and climate chain problems: ‍1. Cardiac HealthAt the University of Oxford, researchers are demonstrating how AI can improve CT scans and their accuracy in detecting artery blockages and narrowing of arteries. AI’s analysis of CT scans outperforms traditional scans, providing a more thorough analysis and greater diagnostic accuracy. It is the case that regular CT scans struggle to pick up on some issues. As a result of AI’s involvement in cardiac imaging, there is a significant improvement in the prescription of appropriate medications and an increase in life-saving treatment earlier. ‍Did you know?Heart disease is the leading cause of death globally, responsible for 1 out of 3 deaths worldwide. AI systems used in cardiac imaging often rely on machine learning algorithms that have been trained on thousands of patient images, allowing these systems to recognize patterns and anomalies that might not be obvious to human observers.AI systems can continuously learn from new data, meaning their diagnostic accuracy could improve over time, potentially leading to more personalized and effective treatment plans for cardiac patients.‍2. Lung Cancer PredictionThe AI tool named “Sybil” created by MIT represents a significant advancement in the fight against lung cancer. From a single low-dose chest scan, Sybil can predict the risk of lung cancer 1-6 years in advance. Its precision surpasses that of human radiologists checking images with the naked eye, and it can identify subtle abnormalities. Sybil has been trained by scientists at MIT using hundreds of CT scans. AstraZeneca and Qure.ai have their own lung cancer detection tool which is 17% more accurate than human radiologists. South Korea’s Lunit is similarly contributing with AI that pinpoints early-stage cancers and predicts patient responses to treatments, an essential step for personalized healthcare. Did you know?Lung cancer is the deadliest cancer worldwide, responsible for 1.7 million deaths in 2020.An early detection results in a 5-year survival rate of approximately ~70%.Advanced lung cancer has a survival rate of only 10%. The UK Government has put aside £21 million in funding for 64 NHS trusts to create AI toolsAI tools like Sybil are trained on hundreds, sometimes thousands, of patient scans, allowing them to learn from diverse patterns of disease manifestation, which significantly enhances their predictive capabilities.3. Drug Discovery ‍AI is speeding up drug discovery. Insilico Medicine’s approach to drug discovery using Generative AI shows how AI is revolutionizing the drug development process for rapid development with reduced risk. Picture this: A protein target that has been associated with a variety of diseases by scientists is attacked using rapidly generated tiny drug molecules that contain properties to target these proteins. Generative AI is then used to predict the outcome of targeting the protein with specific drugs in clinical trials.‍ The failure rate for new treatments is costly, time-consuming, and comes with a high level of risk; AI works to reduce the degree of loss concerning all of these factors. Traditional approaches to drug discovery have a whopping 90% failure rate with a substantive cost of $2.6 billion if we start with a pre-discovered protein target through to clinical trials. This does not even include the decades of research it can take to find the initial protein target. With the advent of AI, the research required to find the protein target can be cut to approximately plus or minus 8 years. “Rather than hunting for molecules with particular effects, Insilico’s AI can design new molecules with the required properties from scratch. One recent example is a potential treatment for fibrosis, a lifelong condition in which scar tissue builds up inside the body. Insilico uses AI to find a safe molecule chemical that targets fibrosis. This drug has undergone Phase I trials, and is now in Phase II. The probability of failure for pharmaceutical development is extremely risky, it’s a molecular casino. But now we’ve managed to demonstrate something that has achieved 1% probability. We took a new novel target that has never been in human clinical trials before, generated novel molecules for it, and took it into human clinical trials and reached Phase II. It is a very low probability event for one company to do internally, especially for an AI company.” -- Alex Zhavoronkov, PhD, founder and CEO of Insilico Medicine Did you know?Once a drug candidate has completed clinical trials, it is submitted to a regulatory agency, such as the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in the United States, for approval. If the drug is approved, it can then be marketed and prescribed to patients. After a drug is approved, it continues to be monitored for safety and efficacy.‍4. Streamlining Documentation Processes The application of Generative AI on large data sets is streamlining the tedious and error-prone documentation processes within healthcare. This AI technology is currently being tested in various aspects of healthcare documentation, such as creating detailed visit summaries and aggregating pertinent research to support patient care decisions. Automating these administrative tasks can significantly reduce the workload of healthcare professionals, allowing them more time to focus on patient care. Did you know?Per-use-fees for implementing and maintaining Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are $1200 per user per yearOne-time upfront implementation costs for setting up an Electronic Health Record (EHR) system can range from $33,000 to $160,000 between smaller and multi-physician practices‍5. Chronic Pain ManagementAn app, called “PainScale”, is being used to determine patterns from patient pain input data – giving insight into the environmental factors such as diet and physical activity which are improving or worsening a person’s pain levels. A tool called “Elipsis” can detect an increase in pain levels by analyzing changes in voice patterns caused by a patient’s fluctuating mental state, which are interrelated.‍‍Did you know?20% of US adults suffer from chronic painAccording to a Pew Research Center Survey, 2 out of 3 U.S. adults stated they would not want AI to determine the quantitative prescription of their pain medication. Despite advances, public opinion on AI in pain treatment reflects caution and resistance.6. Mental Health Support‍The anonymous AI chatbot called “Wysa”, accessible through the app, online, and on WhatsApp, provides mental health support by communicating directly with users through natural language processing and providing evidence-based therapeutic methodologies created by specialist therapists, such as breathing techniques for relaxation and prompts for recentering and reframing deleterious thoughts. It was created to reduce barriers to mental health support. ‍“Wysa started with my own struggle with mental health. As I was struggling, I began to realize that even people like me who are privileged have so many access barriers to mental health and how bad the situation could be for everybody else. And once you see the issue, it’s very hard to unsee it. We started out by trying to create an interface that could use natural language processing and be a place where people could talk about their issues without worrying about being seen by anybody else but still feeling heard. # Price Transparency: Bringing Clarity to the Cost of Health
URL: https://sumhealth.org/post/price-transparency-bringing-clarity-to-the-cost-of-health

Price Transparency: Bringing Clarity to the Cost of HealthDr. Dhruvil PatelFebruary 14, 2024Share to your network Money and Healthcare Within the increasingly complex web of healthcare in the United States, the issue of pricing transparency is more than a matter of fiscal prudence; it's a fundamental right for patients and a cornerstone for fair and competitive services. The labyrinthine nature of hospital pricing systems has long been a source of frustration, confusion, and financial hardship for millions of Americans: from those receiving care to those employed by the system attempting to do right by their patients. In the United States, and in emerging healthcare services around the world the both health and the financial cost of achieving it intertwined. The economics of a healthcare system determine its ability to provide, achieve, and thereby directly impact its outcomes. This focus on cost has emerged a growing recognition of the scale of money that is required to deliver care. Specifically, the cost of delivering healthcare relative to outcomes are completely disproportionate and the burden of such costs directly falls on patients while providers who sanction actions which accumulate them do not have tools to be aware. Due to their vast capacity and resources, such costs are mostly accumulated by patients receiving care from hospital systems whose pricing in the United States is notoriously complex. Unlike most markets where prices are clear and consumers can compare options, healthcare has historically operated with a veil of secrecy over costs. This opacity arises from a mixture of factors including insurance negotiations, service bundling, and the individual nature of patient care which can widely differ on the basis of patient factors. At its core though, healthcare is a service based on the principles of care and well-being. Whenever possible, no patient is turned away and all attempts are made to restore quality of life, no matter the cost - a marked difference from any other industry. Yet patients, often at their most vulnerable, should not be blindsided by bills that are impossible to anticipate. Transparency is a matter of respect and dignity, ensuring that individuals can make informed decisions about their care.‍The Hospital Pricing Transparency LawRecognizing the importance of this issue, there have been legislative efforts at both the federal and state levels to mandate pricing transparency. The Hospital Price Transparency Rule, which took effect in January 2021, requires hospitals to provide clear, accessible pricing information online about the items and services they offer. The development of this law was motivated by the growing concern over escalating healthcare costs and the lack of transparency in hospital pricing. This legislation requires hospitals to publish a comprehensive list of standard charges, including discounted rates negotiated with insurers and the minimum and maximum negotiated charges for all items and services. The primary aim of this law is to enhance price transparency, allowing consumers to make more informed decisions about their healthcare. By making pricing information readily available, the law seeks to foster competition among hospitals, potentially driving down costs and improving service quality. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) is the agency under whose purview it falls to enforce these rules and they have encouraged hospitals to comply to avoid these penalties. The agency also provides guidance and resources to help hospitals meet the requirements of the transparency rule. More information on the transparency rules and its forthcoming updates can be found here [1].Patients often faced significant challenges in understanding the costs associated with their care, leading to unexpected bills and financial hardship. This was a significant step forward, yet implementation and compliance have been inconsistent [2] . Hospitals face challenges in standardizing pricing information due to the complexity of services and the variability of insurance coverage. ‍It Costs More to Not be CompliantFines associated with non-compliance laws vary based on the size of the hospital system and are routinely subject to evolving standards as these laws continue to be updated in concordance with emerging regulations and direction of services involved. CMS has the authority to impose civil monetary penalties on hospitals that do not comply with the price transparency requirements. Specifically:Hospitals could be fined up to $300 per day for non-compliance. This applies to smaller hospitals with a bed count of 30 or fewer.For larger hospitals, the fine can be significantly higher, scaling with the size of the hospital. The maximum penalty can reach up to $5,500 per day, based on the hospital's bed count. This would annualize to a range of $109,500 to $2,007,500 per year, depending on the hospital's size and the duration of non-compliance.‍In our recent review of the CSM public enforcement actions dating to the most recent in September of 2023 - fines for hospital systems in noncompliance with these laws range from $59,100 - $979,000 [3].‍Despite these monetary fines existing, a lack of compliance with this law, coupled by the inconsistent use of web tools designed by health systems for users to access this data has made it increasingly difficult for any participant or facilitator of the healthcare system to understand and make use of it. Yet such transparency can allow for shopping of services by patients, negotiation of prices by providers for their practices, and understanding of cost of care facilitation by adjacent systems: as the hospital transparency law intended. ‍Targeting these problems in healthcare, is exactly what we aim to do here at SumHealth‍Introducing SumHealth Search: Pricing TransparencySumHealth’s Pricing Transparency tool is a comprehensive, searchable database designed to demystify hospital pricing transparency data like never before. This cutting-edge platform not only aggregates data from hospitals nationwide but also presents it in a sleek, user-friendly interface that makes finding and comparing procedure costs straightforward and hassle-free and continuously evolving to provide features for all stakeholders in the care process to use these insights to advocate for an improved care experience. SumHealth's robust healthcare search algorithm and foundation architecture powers this transformative tool, meticulously designed to offer an effective search methodology that navigates the complexities of healthcare pricing with ease. These features ensure that anyone looking for specific procedure costs can find accurate, up-to-date information quickly and effortlessly. The pricing transparency interface boasts an interactable front-end, meticulously crafted to cater to users of all technical abilities. This means that whether you're a patient seeking information on procedure costs or a care system administrator managing vast datasets, SumHealth provides an intuitive, user-friendly experience that simplifies the search and presentation of healthcare pricing data.‍Our Mission is Patient CenteredFor too long, patients have navigated the labyrinth of healthcare pricing with limited information, facing unexpected bills and struggling to make informed decisions about their care. Our platform tackles this challenge head-on by harnessing the power of hospital pricing transparency data, mandated by recent regulations, yet previously scattered and difficult to interpret. With our tool, users can effortlessly search for specific procedures across hospital systems, uncovering not just the sticker price but the actual costs associated with cash payments, gross prices, and the negotiated prices for each insurance provider. This level of detail is unprecedented, empowering patients to make educated choices about their healthcare based on clear, comprehensive cost information.‍Allowing Providers and the Care System to Be More EffectiveThis tool is not solely for just patients. It is also an invaluable resource for healthcare providers, insurers, and care systems. We produced an effective method to remain up-to-date with changes in pricing while working diligently to amass every pricing transparency dataset possible. Presentations of these prices offer insights to help streamline operations, negotiate better rates, and understand the true impact of cost on a patient level - opening the door to improving cost clarity to set patient expectations and improve satisfaction. The platform's seamless integration and customizable presentation tools allow for a tailored approach to data display, ensuring that each care system can showcase their pricing information in a way that aligns with their brand and patient engagement strategies while benefiting from an easy to interact with design interface for quick and effective insights for users. Moreover, our platform's efficiency eliminates the technical challenges of data management involved in curating this data. For care systems, investment in hosting on the SumHealth platform, and using its tools for their efforts in creating an informed healthcare populus signifies a commitment to excellence, demonstrating a proactive approach to the challenges of modern healthcare. # The Current State of Hospital Price Transparency in February 2025
URL: https://sumhealth.org/post/the-current-state-of-hospital-price-transparency-in-february-2025

The Current State of Hospital Price Transparency in February 2025SumHealth TeamFebruary 19, 2025Share to your network Hospital price transparency has become a cornerstone of healthcare reform in the United States, with new regulations taking effect on January 1, 2025. These rules aim to enhance consumer access to pricing information, promote competition, and reduce healthcare costs. However, challenges in compliance and usability persist. Below is an overview of the current state of hospital price transparency.Background and EvolutionHospital price transparency efforts began with the Hospital Price Transparency Rule in 2021, requiring hospitals to publish standard charges for items and services in two formats:A comprehensive machine-readable file.A consumer-friendly display of 300 "shoppable" services 35.Subsequent regulations, such as the No Surprises Act and the Transparency in Coverage Rule, extended similar requirements to insurers and employers, addressing out-of-network billing and providing cost estimates for consumers 23. These measures have laid the groundwork for the stricter standards implemented in 2025.Key Changes in 2025The new requirements introduced in January 2025 represent a significant shift toward greater clarity and accountability:Estimated Allowed Amounts: Hospitals must now publish the average payment they historically receive from third-party payers for each service. This provides consumers with realistic cost benchmarks, particularly for services priced using algorithms or percentages 127.Drug Pricing Information: Hospitals must include detailed drug pricing data, such as unit costs and measurements, as separate elements 110.Compliance Enforcement: CMS has increased penalties for non-compliance, with fines exceeding $2 million annually. Hospitals must also adhere to stricter timelines for correcting errors 47.Compliance LandscapeDespite progress, compliance remains a challenge:A recent Office of Inspector General (OIG) report found that 46% of hospitals were non-compliant with at least one transparency requirement as of early 2023. Common issues included incomplete machine-readable files and poorly formatted consumer-friendly displays 4.Smaller hospitals often struggle due to limited resources, while others cite confusion over regulatory specifics 4.CMS has responded by enhancing enforcement measures, conducting audits, and providing technical assistance to hospitals 34. However, oversight remains limited due to staffing constraints.Impact on Consumers and Healthcare CostsThe potential benefits of price transparency are substantial:Analysts estimate that price transparency could save consumers and insurers between $17.6 billion and $80.7 billion annually by incentivizing cost-effective care choices 6.Tools integrating pricing data into electronic health records (EHRs) and consumer apps are expected to make this information more actionable 10.However, usability remains a concern. While pricing data is increasingly available, its complexity often limits its usefulness for consumers making healthcare decisions 26.Looking AheadThe implementation of the 2025 regulations marks a critical juncture in healthcare reform:Hospitals must prioritize compliance by leveraging technology and aligning with CMS guidelines.Policymakers need to address gaps in oversight and provide ongoing support to ensure the regulations achieve their intended goals.For consumers, continued improvements in data accessibility and usability will be key to realizing the full benefits of price transparency.As these efforts evolve, hospital price transparency has the potential to transform healthcare by fostering competition, reducing costs, and empowering patients with actionable information. However, achieving these outcomes will require sustained collaboration among regulators, providers, and technology developers.‍Sources:https://www.healthcatalyst.com/learn/insights/ready-or-not-hospital-price-transparency-rules-are-herehttps://tendo.com/resources/navigating-price-transparency-in-2025-what-health-systems-need-to-know/https://www.aha.org/fact-sheets/2023-02-24-fact-sheet-hospital-price-transparencyhttps://www.advisory.com/daily-briefing/2024/11/18/price-transparencyhttps://www.cms.gov/priorities/key-initiatives/hospital-price-transparencyhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9940230/https://www.pharmacytimes.com/view/what-to-expect-as-new-cms-hospital-transparency-requirements-take-effect-in-2025https://www.aha.org/news/headline/2024-10-16-cms-hosting-webinar-next-week-price-transparency-machine-readable-file-2025-requirementshttps://www.cms.gov/files/document/cms-hpt-webinar-10-21-2024.pdfhttps://www.cms.gov/files/document/hospital-price-transparency-frequently-asked-questions.pdf You're now subscribed to our newsletter!Oops! # Why Price Transparency Data is Non-Negotiable for Your Self-Insured Plan
URL: https://sumhealth.org/post/why-price-transparency-is-non-negotiable-for-your-self-insured-plan

Why Price Transparency Data is Non-Negotiable for Your Self-Insured PlanSumHealth TeamMay 29, 2025Share to your network Healthcare costs are a perennial headache for businesses, and for self-insured employers, the burden is even more direct. You're not just paying premiums; you're directly responsible for your employees' medical claims. This is why the advent of healthcare price transparency data, mandated for hospitals and payers, is a game-changer for your bottom line and your employees' well-being.No longer are healthcare prices a black box. Hospitals are now required to publish their standard charges, including negotiated rates with insurers, and payers must make their in-network rates and out-of-network allowed amounts publicly accessible. This isn't just a regulatory checkbox; it's a powerful tool for self-insured employers.So, how can you, as a self-insured employer, leverage this wealth of information to your advantage?1. Optimize Your Benefit Design with PrecisionImagine knowing the true cost of an MRI scan at five different imaging centers in your area. This is the power price transparency grants you. Instead of broad strokes, you can now:Design targeted incentives: Encourage employees to choose high-value, lower-cost providers for "shoppable" services like imaging, lab tests, and certain elective surgeries. This could involve lower co-pays or even direct incentives for choosing a preferred, more affordable facility.Tiered networks: Develop benefit designs that steer employees towards more cost-effective providers by categorizing them into tiers based on quality and price.Predictive modeling: Use the data to more accurately forecast your healthcare expenditures, allowing for more precise budgeting and risk management.2. Sharpen Your Negotiation Edge with Payers and TPAsYour relationship with your health plan administrator or Third-Party Administrator (TPA) is crucial. Price transparency data arms you with invaluable intelligence:Benchmark your rates: Are the negotiated rates your TPA is securing for you competitive? By comparing their contracted rates against publicly available data from other payers and providers, you can identify if you're getting the best deal.Challenge inflated costs: If you notice that your plan is consistently paying significantly more for certain procedures than the market average, you have the data to open a conversation with your TPA and push for better terms.Evaluate TPA performance: This data provides a concrete metric to assess how effectively your TPA is managing your healthcare spend. Are they truly leveraging their negotiating power on your behalf?3. Empower Your Employees to Be Smarter Healthcare ConsumersThe most significant impact of price transparency often comes from empowering your workforce. When employees understand the true cost of their care, they can make more informed decisions, directly impacting your claims spend and their out-of-pocket costs:Promote price comparison tools: Educate your employees about available price comparison tools (many payers offer them, and third-party apps are emerging) that utilize this transparency data.Encourage "shoppable" care: Highlight common services where significant price variations exist (e.g., diagnostic imaging, physical therapy, colonoscopies) and encourage employees to shop around.Reduce surprise billing: By understanding in-network vs. out-of-network costs upfront, employees can avoid unexpected bills and disputes. This not only saves money but also reduces employee stress and improves satisfaction.4. Identify and Address Inefficiencies in Your Healthcare SpendTransparency data isn't just about comparing individual prices; it's about uncovering broader patterns and inefficiencies within your healthcare spend:Analyze common claims: Dive into your claims data and cross-reference it with transparency information. Are there specific providers or services where your employees consistently pay more than the market average?Site-of-service optimization: For instance, many diagnostic tests are significantly cheaper when performed in an independent imaging center compared to a hospital outpatient department. You can identify these opportunities and guide employees to lower-cost settings.Identify potential overutilization: While not directly about pricing, understanding where costs are highest can sometimes point to areas of potential overutilization that warrant further investigation.Getting Started: Your Action PlanLeveraging price transparency data might seem daunting, but here's how to begin:Educate yourself and your team: Understand the types of data available and how to access it.Partner with your TPA: Demand that your TPA provides you with actionable insights derived from this data. They should be your partner in cost containment.Explore third-party solutions: Several companies specialize in aggregating and analyzing this complex data, offering user-friendly dashboards and actionable recommendations.Communicate with your employees: Make it easy for them to access and understand price information. The more informed they are, the more they can help you save.The era of opaque healthcare pricing is ending. For self-insured employers, this shift presents an unprecedented opportunity to take control of your healthcare costs, optimize your benefits, and empower your employees to make smarter, more cost-effective healthcare decisions. Embrace the data, and unlock significant savings.At SumHealth, we're at the forefront of healthcare price transparency, transforming raw, often overwhelming, pricing files into clear, actionable intelligence. Our specialized solutions, built on deep policy and clinical expertise, empower self-insured employers like you to precisely benchmark costs, optimize your benefit plans, and finally understand what you're truly paying for healthcare services. Don't let your competitors get ahead. Connect with SumHealth today to unlock the hidden value in price transparency data and start realizing significant, measurable savings for your organization and your employees. Is Your Hospital Ready for CMS Pricing Transparency?
Our team of compliance experts is here to help

pages.bodyText: Navigate CMS Pricing Transparency Mandates with ConfidenceEnsure Full Compliance and Avoid Penalties with SumHealth.Schedule DemoView FAQsLET US HELPIs Your Hospital Ready for CMS Pricing Transparency?The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) mandates for hospital pricing transparency are more than just guidelines – they're a critical compliance imperative. pages.title: A Quick Glimpse into Healthcare Price Transparency Efforts
pages.metaDescription: Discover the latest efforts pushing for greater healthcare price transparency. This post explores recent legislation, initiatives, and innovative tech solutions like SumHealth's platform that are empowering patients with clear pricing data.

headings.text: A Quick Glimpse into Healthcare Price Transparency Efforts

pages.bodyText: A Quick Glimpse into Healthcare Price Transparency EffortsMarch 18, 2024Share to your network For too long, patients in the United States have been left in the dark when it comes to understanding the true costs of healthcare services. The lack of price transparency has been a major contributor to rising healthcare costs and patient frustration. However, recent efforts and legislation aim to lift the veil on medical billing practices, empowering consumers with crucial pricing information.‍GroundworkThe push for healthcare price transparency gained momentum in the late 2010s. The Hospital Price Transparency rule, which took effect in January 2021, required hospitals to disclose their negotiated rates with insurers and the cash prices for various services. While this was a step in the right direction, the rule faced challenges in implementation and enforcement, leaving many patients still perplexed by the complex maze of healthcare pricing.‍Existing Legislation and InitiativesIn addition to the Hospital Price Transparency rule, several other legislative efforts and initiatives have aimed to improve price transparency in the healthcare sector:‍The No Surprises Act (2022):This legislation aimed to protect patients from unexpected medical bills from out-of-network providers, particularly in emergency situations.It also required healthcare providers to furnish patients with good-faith estimates of expected charges for scheduled services.‍State-Level Efforts:Several states, such as Colorado, Connecticut, and Nevada, have implemented their own price transparency laws, requiring healthcare providers to disclose pricing information to consumers.Insurer Transparency Initiatives:Some insurance companies have started to provide pricing tools and cost estimators to their members, allowing them to compare costs for various services across different providers.‍Potential Impact and ChallengesImproved healthcare price transparency has the potential to empower patients and drive down costs through increased competition and informed decision-making. When armed with pricing information, consumers can shop around for the most affordable options, putting pressure on healthcare providers to offer competitive rates.However, implementing effective transparency measures has proven challenging. Resistance from industry stakeholders, concerns about the accuracy and usability of pricing information, and the sheer complexity of healthcare billing practices have all presented obstacles.‍Moving ForwardAs the push for healthcare price transparency continues, policymakers, industry leaders, and consumer advocates must work together to address the remaining challenges. Efforts should focus on ensuring pricing information is not only disclosed but also presented in a clear, standardized, and easily understandable manner for patients.Ultimately, greater transparency in healthcare costs is crucial for building a more consumer-friendly and affordable healthcare system. By shining a light on often-hidden expenses, patients can make informed decisions about their care and take control of their healthcare expenses.‍‍Our Contribution to Healthcare Cost Transparency‍At SumHealth, we recognize the growing importance of healthcare price transparency and the need to empower patients with clear, accessible pricing information. That's why we've developed an innovative platform designed to simplify the process of displaying and searching hospital price data. Currently available to healthcare providers and businesses, our platform will soon be rolled out to regular consumers as well.With our user-friendly solution, consumers will be able to easily research and compare costs across different hospitals and healthcare facilities. By bringing pricing data into the open and making it easily searchable, we aim to bridge the information gap and contribute to greater cost clarity within the healthcare industry.Our platform represents a technological solution to a long-standing problem – the lack of transparency around healthcare costs. As the push for price transparency continues to gain momentum, SumHealth is proud to be at the forefront, leveraging technology to put powerful pricing information directly into the hands of patients and caregivers.We encourage you to stay tuned for updates on the consumer launch of our hospital price data platform. Together, we can foster a more transparent, consumer-friendly healthcare landscape. You're now subscribed to our newsletter!Oops! pages.title: An Analysis of Payer Machine-Readable Files (MRFs) and the Challenges of Healthcare Price Transparency
pages.metaDescription: Explore the complexities of payer MRFs: size, compliance, and data challenges. Learn how to navigate these files for healthcare price transparency and discover solutions.

headings.text: An Analysis of Payer Machine-Readable Files (MRFs) and the Challenges of Healthcare Price Transparency
Conceptual Framework: The Purpose of MRFs
Quantitative Analysis: The Scale of Data Volume
Qualitative Assessment: Structural Complexity and Data Heterogeneity
Compliance Evaluation: Adherence to Regulatory Standards

pages.bodyText: An Analysis of Payer Machine-Readable Files (MRFs) and the Challenges of Healthcare Price TransparencySumHealth TeamMarch 6, 2025Share to your network The implementation of the Transparency in Coverage (TiC) rule, mandated by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), has introduced a paradigm shift in healthcare price transparency. At the core of this initiative are Machine-Readable Files (MRFs), comprehensive datasets published by health insurers detailing negotiated rates for in-network services. However, the practical application of these files has revealed significant challenges related to data volume, structural complexity, and compliance variability.Conceptual Framework: The Purpose of MRFsMRFs are designed to fulfill the TiC rule's objective of empowering consumers with access to price information, thereby facilitating informed healthcare decision-making. These files encompass:In-network rate files: Articulating negotiated reimbursement rates between insurers and contracted providers.Allowed amount files: Presenting out-of-network allowed amounts, indicating the insurer's determination of reasonable charges.Prescription drug files: Disclosing negotiated rates and historical net prices for pharmaceutical products.These files are intended to serve as a foundational resource for price discovery and comparative analysis.Quantitative Analysis: The Scale of Data VolumeA primary impediment to MRF utilization is the sheer magnitude of the data. Observations include:Data Set Size: Files routinely exceed gigabytes and, in some instances, terabytes, requiring substantial computational resources for processing.Variability in Scale: Data volume fluctuates significantly based on insurer size, network breadth, and the extent of negotiated rate agreements.Temporal Data Accumulation: Monthly updates compound the existing data load, necessitating scalable data management solutions.This quantitative scale presents a significant challenge for researchers and developers seeking to derive meaningful insights.Qualitative Assessment: Structural Complexity and Data HeterogeneityBeyond volume, the intrinsic complexity of MRF data structures poses a substantial barrier to effective analysis. Key challenges include:Data Format and Parsing: Files are typically formatted in JSON or other machine-readable formats, demanding specialized parsing capabilities.Lack of Data Standardization: While CMS provides guidelines, variations in data organization and nomenclature across insurers impede cross-insurer comparisons.Medical Coding and Terminology: The use of complex medical coding systems and terminology necessitates domain expertise for accurate interpretation.Granularity and Data Abstraction: While detailed data is provided, the level of granularity can obfuscate the overall cost of specific services, requiring sophisticated data abstraction techniques.Compliance Evaluation: Adherence to Regulatory StandardsCompliance with the TiC rule has exhibited variability across insurers. Observations include:Accessibility and Discoverability: Some files are not readily accessible or discoverable, hindering public access.Data Completeness and Accuracy: Instances of incomplete or inaccurate data have been reported, raising concerns about data integrity.Timeliness of Publication: Adherence to the mandated monthly publication schedule has proven challenging for some insurers.Regulatory Enforcement: The efficacy of regulatory enforcement mechanisms remains a subject of ongoing discussion.Implications and Future DirectionsThe TiC rule and MRFs hold the potential to transform healthcare price transparency. However, the current challenges necessitate concerted efforts to:Establish standardized data formats and nomenclature.Develop robust data analysis tools and platforms.Enhance regulatory oversight and enforcement.Promote interdisciplinary research to analyze the data and create useful tools.By addressing these challenges, we can realize the full potential of MRFs in fostering a more transparent and efficient healthcare market. Organizations seeking to navigate the complexities of payer data can leverage specialized services to streamline their analysis and decision-making processes. For instance, SumHealth provides comprehensive payer data services, offering expertise in accessing, processing, and interpreting MRF data. These services empower organizations to gain valuable insights into market trends, pricing dynamics, and competitive landscapes, ultimately facilitating informed strategic planning and operational efficiency. You can explore SumHealth's payer data services further at https://www.sumhealth.org/payer-data. You're now subscribed to our newsletter!Oops! pages.bodyText: Hospital Price Transparency 2025: Your Complete Compliance Guide to Avoid Millions in PenaltiesSumHealth TeamJuly 28, 2025Share to your network With only 21% of hospitals fully compliant and a new executive order mandating aggressive enforcement, your hospital could face up to $5.5 million in annual penalties. Here's your urgent action plan.The $5.5 Million Question: Is Your Hospital Ready?As of July 2025, a shocking 79% of U.S. hospitals remain non-compliant with federal price transparency regulations. With President Trump's February 2025 executive order "Making America Healthy Again" mandating immediate enforcement action, hospitals face an unprecedented compliance crisis.Key Facts That Should Keep Hospital Leaders Awake:46% of hospitals failed basic compliance requirements in recent OIG auditsDaily penalties now range from $300 to $5,500 based on bed count90-day compliance window strictly enforced as of 2025Public naming of non-compliant hospitals damages reputationCriminal penalties potentially on the horizon per new enforcement guidanceBreaking: What Changed in 2025The February Executive Order ImpactThe game changed dramatically on February 25, 2025. SumHealth's Price Transparency Solution: Fast Track Compliance

pages.bodyText: February 2024 Hospital Price Transparency Report Reveals Widespread NoncomplianceApril 9, 2024Share to your network The latest Hospital Price Transparency Compliance Report, released in February 2024, sheds light on the progress and challenges in achieving meaningful price transparency in the healthcare industry. This report, compiled by PatientRightsAdvocate.org, analyzed the websites of 2,000 U.S. hospitals to assess their compliance with the federal Hospital Price Transparency Rule. Download the full report here.Key Highlights:1. Compliance Rates Remain Low: Only 34.5% of the hospitals reviewed were found to be fully compliant with all the requirements of the price transparency rule. The remaining 65.5% were deemed noncompliant due to missing or incomplete pricing data.2. Inconsistent Reporting: The report found significant variation in compliance among the nation's largest hospital systems. While some systems, like Community Health Systems and Christus Health, had high compliance rates, others, such as HCA Healthcare, Tenet Healthcare, and Kaiser Permanente, had no fully compliant hospitals.3. Barriers to Usability: Many hospitals posted pricing data in formats that were difficult for consumers and technology developers to navigate, such as unnecessarily large "tall" files and complex JSON-formatted files without user documentation.4. Lack of Enforcement: The report highlights the need for more thorough and timely enforcement of the price transparency rule by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS). To date, CMS has issued penalty notices to only 14 out of the approximately 1,800 hospitals it has identified as noncompliant.Insights and Recommendations:1. Strengthen Enforcement: The report calls for CMS to enforce the price transparency rule more quickly and thoroughly, publicly post the entire compliance process, and utilize payers' Transparency in Coverage data to verify the accuracy and completeness of hospitals' price files.2. Eliminate Price Estimates: The report suggests that CMS should require hospitals to provide actual, binding prices for each payer and plan, as well as discounted cash prices, rather than relying on non-binding price estimates.3. Standardize Pricing Data: The report recommends that CMS set additional requirements for the "tall" file format, including a maximum number of rows and a cap on file size, to improve the usability of the data.4. Require Executive Attestation: The report encourages CMS to require senior hospital officials, such as the Chief Executive Officer, to attest to the accuracy and completeness of the pricing data, potentially incorporating liability under the False Claims Act.5. Expand Transparency to All Providers: The report suggests that price transparency requirements should be expanded to all healthcare providers, including ambulatory surgical centers and accountable care organizations, to allow for more comprehensive price comparisons.SumHealth's Price Transparency Solution: Fast Track ComplianceSumHealth offers a dynamic price transparency solution, swiftly tailored to meet the unique needs of any hospital. This adaptable solution ensures a rapid and efficient customization process, allowing hospitals to seamlessly integrate comprehensive transparency features designed specifically for their individual requirements.To learn more about how SumHealth can help your hospital navigate price transparency compliance, contact us at contact@sumhealth.org or schedule a demo here.‍ You're now subscribed to our newsletter!Oops! pages.title: How AI Is Transforming Healthcare
pages.metaDescription: Artificial intelligence (AI) and Generative AI are on track to adding trillions of dollars to the global economy through an increase in worker productivity by 0.1% to 0.6% into 2040 according to statistics by McKinsey. In particular, we see the impact of AI on global healthcare, offering solutions from advanced diagnostics to the management of chronic conditions.
pages.bodyText: How AI Is Transforming HealthcareZara AhmedMay 8, 2024Share to your network Artificial intelligence (AI) and Generative AI are on track to adding trillions of dollars to the global economy through an increase in worker productivity by 0.1% to 0.6% into 2040 according to statistics by McKinsey. In particular, we see the impact of AI on global healthcare, offering solutions from advanced diagnostics to the management of chronic conditions. 38% of Americans think AI usage in healthcare would improve patient outcomes according to a Pew Research Center Survey. Here are six ways that AI is reshaping global healthcare systems, and reducing pressure on them due to supply chain and climate chain problems: ‍1. Cardiac HealthAt the University of Oxford, researchers are demonstrating how AI can improve CT scans and their accuracy in detecting artery blockages and narrowing of arteries. AI’s analysis of CT scans outperforms traditional scans, providing a more thorough analysis and greater diagnostic accuracy. It is the case that regular CT scans struggle to pick up on some issues. As a result of AI’s involvement in cardiac imaging, there is a significant improvement in the prescription of appropriate medications and an increase in life-saving treatment earlier. ‍Did you know?Heart disease is the leading cause of death globally, responsible for 1 out of 3 deaths worldwide. AI systems used in cardiac imaging often rely on machine learning algorithms that have been trained on thousands of patient images, allowing these systems to recognize patterns and anomalies that might not be obvious to human observers.AI systems can continuously learn from new data, meaning their diagnostic accuracy could improve over time, potentially leading to more personalized and effective treatment plans for cardiac patients.‍2. Lung Cancer PredictionThe AI tool named “Sybil” created by MIT represents a significant advancement in the fight against lung cancer. From a single low-dose chest scan, Sybil can predict the risk of lung cancer 1-6 years in advance. Its precision surpasses that of human radiologists checking images with the naked eye, and it can identify subtle abnormalities. Sybil has been trained by scientists at MIT using hundreds of CT scans. AstraZeneca and Qure.ai have their own lung cancer detection tool which is 17% more accurate than human radiologists. South Korea’s Lunit is similarly contributing with AI that pinpoints early-stage cancers and predicts patient responses to treatments, an essential step for personalized healthcare. Did you know?Lung cancer is the deadliest cancer worldwide, responsible for 1.7 million deaths in 2020.An early detection results in a 5-year survival rate of approximately ~70%.Advanced lung cancer has a survival rate of only 10%. The UK Government has put aside £21 million in funding for 64 NHS trusts to create AI toolsAI tools like Sybil are trained on hundreds, sometimes thousands, of patient scans, allowing them to learn from diverse patterns of disease manifestation, which significantly enhances their predictive capabilities.3. Drug Discovery ‍AI is speeding up drug discovery. Insilico Medicine’s approach to drug discovery using Generative AI shows how AI is revolutionizing the drug development process for rapid development with reduced risk. Picture this: A protein target that has been associated with a variety of diseases by scientists is attacked using rapidly generated tiny drug molecules that contain properties to target these proteins. Generative AI is then used to predict the outcome of targeting the protein with specific drugs in clinical trials.‍ The failure rate for new treatments is costly, time-consuming, and comes with a high level of risk; AI works to reduce the degree of loss concerning all of these factors. Traditional approaches to drug discovery have a whopping 90% failure rate with a substantive cost of $2.6 billion if we start with a pre-discovered protein target through to clinical trials. This does not even include the decades of research it can take to find the initial protein target. With the advent of AI, the research required to find the protein target can be cut to approximately plus or minus 8 years. “Rather than hunting for molecules with particular effects, Insilico’s AI can design new molecules with the required properties from scratch. One recent example is a potential treatment for fibrosis, a lifelong condition in which scar tissue builds up inside the body. Insilico uses AI to find a safe molecule chemical that targets fibrosis. This drug has undergone Phase I trials, and is now in Phase II. The probability of failure for pharmaceutical development is extremely risky, it’s a molecular casino. But now we’ve managed to demonstrate something that has achieved 1% probability. We took a new novel target that has never been in human clinical trials before, generated novel molecules for it, and took it into human clinical trials and reached Phase II. It is a very low probability event for one company to do internally, especially for an AI company.” -- Alex Zhavoronkov, PhD, founder and CEO of Insilico Medicine Did you know?Once a drug candidate has completed clinical trials, it is submitted to a regulatory agency, such as the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in the United States, for approval. If the drug is approved, it can then be marketed and prescribed to patients. After a drug is approved, it continues to be monitored for safety and efficacy.‍4. Streamlining Documentation Processes The application of Generative AI on large data sets is streamlining the tedious and error-prone documentation processes within healthcare. This AI technology is currently being tested in various aspects of healthcare documentation, such as creating detailed visit summaries and aggregating pertinent research to support patient care decisions. Automating these administrative tasks can significantly reduce the workload of healthcare professionals, allowing them more time to focus on patient care. Did you know?Per-use-fees for implementing and maintaining Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are $1200 per user per yearOne-time upfront implementation costs for setting up an Electronic Health Record (EHR) system can range from $33,000 to $160,000 between smaller and multi-physician practices‍5. Chronic Pain ManagementAn app, called “PainScale”, is being used to determine patterns from patient pain input data – giving insight into the environmental factors such as diet and physical activity which are improving or worsening a person’s pain levels. A tool called “Elipsis” can detect an increase in pain levels by analyzing changes in voice patterns caused by a patient’s fluctuating mental state, which are interrelated.‍‍Did you know?20% of US adults suffer from chronic painAccording to a Pew Research Center Survey, 2 out of 3 U.S. adults stated they would not want AI to determine the quantitative prescription of their pain medication. Despite advances, public opinion on AI in pain treatment reflects caution and resistance.6. Mental Health Support‍The anonymous AI chatbot called “Wysa”, accessible through the app, online, and on WhatsApp, provides mental health support by communicating directly with users through natural language processing and providing evidence-based therapeutic methodologies created by specialist therapists, such as breathing techniques for relaxation and prompts for recentering and reframing deleterious thoughts. It was created to reduce barriers to mental health support. ‍“Wysa started with my own struggle with mental health. As I was struggling, I began to realize that even people like me who are privileged have so many access barriers to mental health and how bad the situation could be for everybody else. And once you see the issue, it’s very hard to unsee it. We started out by trying to create an interface that could use natural language processing and be a place where people could talk about their issues without worrying about being seen by anybody else but still feeling heard. We began to realize that it’s reaching so many people who otherwise have no access to mental health support and it’s really helping them.” ‍ -- Jo Aggarwal, CEO of Wysa Wysa has reached millions of users worldwide and continues to expand its language offerings to extend its reach and impact. Did you know?A tool called “Elipsis” can detect unreported mental health issues by analyzing changes in speech patterns caused by a patient’s fluctuating mental stateAccording to the World Health Organization (WHO), roughly 1 in 8 people in the world live with a mental disorderMental health conditions have a substantial economic cost. The WHO estimates that the global economy loses over US$ 1 trillion per year in productivity due to depression and anxiety alone‍ You're now subscribed to our newsletter!Oops!
